footer.bio: Senior Technical PM. I prototype in code, design the UX, and ship.

hero.headline: Senior Technical Product Manager. Design, code, and product management in the same head.
hero.subtitle: B2B SaaS · Payments · Platform. 10+ years designing interfaces, writing production code, and managing products. The advantage: when design, engineering, and business don't understand each other, no translator needed.
hero.signaturePhrase: The problem is never the one from the first meeting.

howIWork.label: HOW I WORK
howIWork.title: How I work
howIWork.intro: When design says "the user gets stuck here because the visual hierarchy is wrong," engineering says "we need 3 sprints of refactoring," and business says "it's costing us €15K a month in support tickets." Three separate conversations. My job is to turn them into one decision.

pillar1.title: Designer + Developer + PM. In the same room.
pillar1.text: 4 years designing interfaces. 4 writing production code. 5+ managing products and cross-functional teams of up to 10+ people. When design and engineering don't understand each other, the problem usually isn't technical. It's that nobody in the room has done both jobs. Weekly cross-functional sessions cut post-release incidents by 25%. Less rework, fewer meetings, decisions that stick.
pillar1.shortText: 4 years design. 4 code. 5+ product. When design and engineering don't understand each other, the problem usually isn't technical. Cross-functional sessions cut post-release incidents by 25%.

pillar2.title: The problem is never the one from the first meeting
pillar2.text: An example. Payment system in crisis, 44 open problems. The pressure is "act on everything, now." Two weeks talking to support, reading tickets, looking at the data. Result: 12 critical, 4 with immediate financial impact. The rest could wait. The 4 most severe resolved in 6 weeks, not 6 months. Before building anything, verify you're solving the right problem.
pillar2.shortText: Payment system in crisis, 44 open problems. Two weeks of discovery: 12 critical, 4 with financial impact. The 4 most severe resolved in 6 weeks, not 6 months.

pillar3.title: Prototypes, not slides
pillar3.text: A React prototype users can touch says more than any spec document. React, TypeScript, and Node.js to validate an idea before asking the team for months of development. Priority comes from user frustration, not technical complexity. And when the data says a feature isn't needed, the answer is no. Even when 60% of users are asking for it.
pillar3.shortText: A React prototype users can touch says more than any spec. Priority from user frustration, not technical complexity. When data says no, the answer is no.

timeline.label: THE PATH
timeline.title: The path

block1.text: A web design studio launched at 25 in northern Italy. 20+ clients, everything in one person: sales, design, development, delivery, support. First e-commerce site: award-worthy interface, zero sales. The rule that came out of it still holds.
block1.shortText: Web design studio. 20+ clients, everything in one person. First e-commerce: award-worthy interface, zero sales.

block2.text: Joined as a UX Designer, left writing production code. B2B and B2C products for clients including Prometeia, Casavo, CliensPiu. CliensPiu: a legal practice management system used 8+ hours a day, designed and built by the same person. After 4 years of designing and then implementing the same product, the line between the two disciplines stopped making sense.
block2.shortText: UX Designer → production code. B2B/B2C products including CliensPiù — designed and built by the same person.

block3.text: LeadsBridge: B2B SaaS platform with 380+ connectors, Meta, Google, LinkedIn, TikTok partnerships. Solo PM, fully remote from Europe. The core flow redesign cut setup time by 35% and unlocked revenue growth.
block3.shortText: B2B SaaS, 380+ connectors. Solo PM, fully remote. Core flow redesign cut setup time by 35%.

block4.text: Payment systems, platform integrations, and cashless solutions for centers across 5 countries. A system inherited in critical state. Today: 99%+ uptime, -25% post-release incidents, 116 centers integrated, team of 10+.
block4.shortText: Payments, platform, cashless. 99%+ uptime, -25% incidents, 116 centers, team of 10+.

timeline.closingLine: Today when I talk to a designer, I understand the constraints. When I talk to a developer, I understand the trade-offs. When I talk to business, I translate.

metric1.label: Uptime
metric1.context: Payment uptime and success rate — QubicaAMF. From daily crises to enterprise stability.

metric2.label: Post-release incidents
metric2.context: Post-release incidents — QubicaAMF. Weekly cross-functional sessions with Product, Support, and Engineering.

metric4.label: Setup time
metric4.context: Setup time — LeadsBridge. Core product flow redesign.

metric5.label: Integration adoption
metric5.context: Integration adoption — QubicaAMF. 47-page PDFs replaced with 3-minute videos.

metric6.label: Processing times
metric6.context: Payment processing times — QubicaAMF. Full payment pipeline optimization.

card1.title: Payments and platform at international scale
card1.text: Payment systems, platform integrations, and cashless solutions for centers across 5 countries. Next step: bringing AI into the operational flow to reduce manual reconciliation and support workload.

card2.title: A personal problem turned product
card2.text: Finding an apartment across 8 listing sites by hand wasn't working. Automated scraping, smart scoring, 880+ listings analyzed. The tool is still running.

card3.title: Personal finance without spreadsheets
card3.text: Automatic transaction analysis, pattern recognition, weekly insights. Designing an interface that doesn't ask you to fill out rows and columns.

contact.label: LET'S TALK
contact.title: Let's talk
contact.text: If you think my profile could work for your team, write me. Even if you're not sure it's the right fit. The best conversations I've had started with "I'm not sure this is your thing, but..."
contact.availability: EU remote . US remote . Italy hybrid

about.headline: Better products happen when one person speaks all three languages.
about.subtitle: I didn't start in product management. I got here through design, code, and a lot of building. Here's how.

selfrules.text:
A web design studio launched at 25 in northern Italy. For 7 years, everything went through one person: sales, design, development, delivery, support. 20+ clients.

The first e-commerce site had an award-worthy interface and zero sales. The customer couldn't find the checkout button. €8K in refunds. When you sell, design, build, and support the same product, cross-functional thinking isn't optional. And one rule becomes permanent: if it's beautiful but confusing, it's decoration.

flowing.text:
Joined as a UX Designer, left writing production code. B2B and B2C products for clients including Prometeia, Casavo, CliensPiù.

The most formative project: CliensPiù, a legal practice management system used 8+ hours a day. Every micro-interaction matters when someone lives in the tool. Designing the interface, then writing the code that implements it. After 4 years of doing both, the line between "the designer" and "the developer" stopped making sense.

leadsbridge.text:
LeadsBridge: B2B SaaS platform with 380+ connectors and direct partnerships with Meta, Google, LinkedIn, and TikTok. Solo PM, fully remote from Europe, team of 10.

The product's core flow was also its weakest point — the kind of problem everyone had accepted as normal. The redesign cut setup time by <bold>35%</bold> and directly unlocked revenue growth.

qubicamf.text:
600 employees, 90+ countries. Payment systems, platform integrations, and cashless solutions for entertainment centers. A system inherited in critical state. How it was stabilized is a story worth its own case study.

Today: a team of 10+ across backend, QA, and analysts. <bold>99%+</bold> uptime, <bold>-25%</bold> post-release incidents, <bold>116 centers</bold> integrated, <bold>5 new per week</bold>. Stabilization was step one. The current work is strategic: what to build, what not to build, and why.

belief1.title: The problem is never the one from the first meeting.
belief1.text: The first meeting tells you the symptom. The real work is digging to the cause. Sometimes that means saying "the solution we're evaluating doesn't solve the real problem." It doesn't win you friends in the first meeting. By the third, they agree.

belief2.title: A working prototype beats 100 slides.
belief2.text: An idea, before becoming a ticket, becomes a working prototype. React that users can touch says more than any spec document. The data you need to decide comes from the field, not from meetings.

belief3.title: The best PM is the one the team doesn't notice.
belief3.text: If the team works well, credit goes to the team. But "removing obstacles" isn't enough. The real work is figuring out what to build and why. Giving the team a direction that holds even when priorities shift. Protecting focus isn't saying "no" to interruptions. It's having done the strategic work upfront, so the team already knows what matters. If the PM becomes the bottleneck between builders and decision-makers, something went wrong.

outside.text: Based in northern Italy. When not working, probably at the gym, building something with LEGO, or trying something outside the comfort zone. Latest experiment: embroidery. Will go back eventually, but not before figuring out what's on the other side.

cta.text: If your product needs someone who gets their hands in it — not just words on top — let's talk. Even just to see if it makes sense.

work.headline: How I work, demonstrated.
work.intro: Two product stories. The context, the challenge, the approach, the numbers. Not what someone says they can do. What happened.

payments.title: A payment system losing transactions every day
payments.preview: Hundreds of centers across 5 countries, 44 open issues with no priority, 4 with immediate financial impact. Stabilization without rewriting anything.

cashless.title: Cashless from zero: three providers, an "unsolvable" problem, and a product to park
cashless.preview: The internal product looked like the obvious choice. Discovery told a different story. How the cashless strategy was defined by working from real constraints.
cashless.note: Full metrics available upon production launch — pilot data currently being collected

cta.text: These cases tell the how. For the who, there's the about page. If you want to talk, write me.

payments.company: B2B SaaS platform · Entertainment & leisure · 5 countries

context.heading: The context
content: A B2B platform serving hundreds of entertainment centers across five countries. The primary payment integration, built on Square, was the fastest-growing product in the portfolio: **42% of total transaction volume**, **25% year-over-year growth**, the only provider trending upward.

Then the growth outran the product. The original MVP had been designed for a handful of friendly early adopters. By mid-2025 it was running in production across a much broader customer base, and the gaps between “technically functional” and “operationally usable” were becoming visible to everyone.

challenge.heading: The challenge
content: Over **60% of recently onboarded customers** were either unhappy or had paused implementation. Social media complaints were compounding. Centers in production were experiencing double charges, payment timeouts during peak hours, broken split payments, and missing refunds. Each of these had direct financial impact on center operations.

The deeper problem was structural. New centers were still being onboarded at full pace while existing ones were struggling. Every new unhappy customer added complaints, reputation damage, and support load. The product had outrun its capabilities, and growth was making it worse.
There was no consolidated view of what was broken, no shared severity ranking, and no single owner driving resolution.

approach.heading: The approach
content: **The first decision was to stop scaling the problem.** Growth features went on hold. Customer acquisition slowed down. The entire team shifted to a single priority: make the centers already in production work reliably. This required aligning sales leadership, engineering, and executive stakeholders around the same data. The customer satisfaction numbers made the case.

**Then came triage.** 44 open items sat in the backlog, compiled from engineering, sales, and support with no ranking. The sorting criterion was simple: is this costing a center money right now? That split the backlog into three tiers. 12 items were critical to payments operations. Of those, 4 had immediate financial impact on live centers: double charges, payment timeouts, broken split payments, missing refunds. The remaining 32 were real issues, but they could wait.
The 4 highest-impact items got one owner each, a deadline, and a published commitment. All four shipped within six weeks. Zero complaints on any of them post-deployment.

**While the team stabilized, I dug into a problem others had classified as unsolvable.** A major center was about to cancel the integration because they couldn't reconcile their books. Every transaction appeared as “uncategorized income” in the Square dashboard. No breakdown by terminal, no source attribution, no way to close the books.

The root cause was in the Square API. The field where we stored transaction metadata didn't preserve enough information. I traced the data model until I found `external_payment_type`, an existing parameter that could carry source attribution without architectural changes on either side. I wrote the implementation spec and handed it to the team. The center stayed and became a reference account.

**Scope discipline mattered as much as speed.** Over the full year, 23 of the 44 items shipped. The other 21 were deliberate “not now” decisions, each with a documented rationale. The 12 critical payments items were brought down to 5 remaining, with every resolved issue validated in production. When the refund feature wasn't solid enough before the holiday peak, I pulled it back and ran a full testing day rather than ship broken logic to hundreds of centers during their busiest week.

**Parallel to stabilization, two compliance threads ran.** I proposed a scope reduction for PCI DSS certification by presenting the certifiers with a three-channel payment architecture, removing significant recurring penalty risk. I also completed the FreedomPay technical certification end-to-end, which unlocked a new segment of enterprise centers.

**Communication was deliberate.** Structured weekly recaps, operational memos for urgent decisions, meeting minutes published within hours. When the support team needed to help customers with issues that weren't fixed yet, I built video tutorials covering workarounds. Bridge solutions while the real fixes were in the pipeline.

items.label: processing times across all payment flows
global success rate. 100% uptime against a 99% target
YoY growth for the Square integration, the only payment provider still growing
centers integrated. 5 new centers per week onboarding pace
backlog items shipped. Critical payments issues reduced from 12 to 5 remaining

learned.heading: What I learned
content: The instinct in a crisis is to fix and grow at the same time. It doesn't work. The first real product decision is to stop making the hole deeper. Everything else follows from that.

The second thing: 44 items, 12 critical, 4 urgent. Most of what felt urgent could actually wait. Knowing which ones can't is the actual skill. A single clear sorting criterion gave the team more clarity than any amount of discussion.

The third: when a problem gets labeled “unsolvable” or “waiting on the partner,” it's worth checking whether someone has actually read the API documentation. The source attribution fix was already there. It just needed someone willing to look.

cashless.company: B2B SaaS platform · Entertainment & leisure · 5 countries
cashless.secondMetricLabel: product killed before it became a liability

context.heading: The context
content: A B2B platform where the payments product was growing fast. Square integration covered **42% of transaction volume**, centers were onboarding at **5 per week**, and the core payment flows were stable.

But customers kept asking the same question: when can we go cashless? The industry was moving toward card-based game play, stored-value wallets, and tap-to-play systems. The main competitor already had a unified cashless offering, and that gap was showing up in every competitive deal.

The problem wasn't whether to build cashless. The problem was that no integration existed between cashless providers and the platform's payment architecture, and the technical gap was deeper than anyone had scoped.

challenge.heading: The challenge
content: Cashless systems work on a deposit-and-redeem model. A customer loads money onto a card, then spends it at game terminals. Simple from the user's side. From the accounting side, a mess: the deposit registers as income, and the redemption registers as a second income. Double counting. Every center running cashless would need to reconcile manually, or their books wouldn't close.

Three cashless providers were on the table. Each had different API architectures, different pricing models, and different levels of willingness to co-develop. No one internally had mapped the landscape or defined what “cashless integration” actually meant for the platform.

approach.heading: The approach
content: **The first move was to understand what we were actually building.** Not “a cashless feature” but a specific product definition: what does cashless integration mean for a platform that already runs Square payments? What's the data model? Where does the money flow? I mapped the full transaction lifecycle across deposit, play, and redemption before evaluating any provider.

**Then I killed the obvious option.** The internal product, OneCashless, had been explored through multiple discovery sessions. On paper it looked like the fastest path. In practice, the product wasn't convincing. The business model was unclear, the technical architecture required work we couldn't justify, and forcing a weak product into production would have created the same crisis the payments team had just spent months fixing. I parked it. Deliberately.

**Intercard was the first real test.** A $500-per-terminal API fee, but they had existing market presence. I scoped a proof of concept with two pilot centers, got it validated, and put it into daily use. It proved cashless integration was technically viable on the platform.

**The real unlock was Amusement Connect.** They had something no other provider offered: native compatibility with Square. That meant the double-counting problem had a structural solution, not just a workaround. I initiated a co-development partnership and negotiated a shared roadmap toward feature parity with the Intercard integration.

**The double-counting problem got a specific solution.** I explored multiple approaches: QR-based app payments, gift card mechanisms, physical hardware swaps. None solved it cleanly. The breakthrough was a payout-based model using Square's gift card infrastructure, where redemptions offset deposits instead of creating new income lines. Clean books. No manual reconciliation.

**BowlExpo validated the direction.** The industry's largest trade show, and the platform needed to show cashless capability. The team delivered a working integration in under five months from kickoff. Not a demo. A working system.

items.label: pilot centers validated on Intercard, proving platform viability
co-development partnership established with shared roadmap
from zero to live cashless demo at the industry's largest trade show
product killed (OneCashless) before it could become a liability

learned.heading: What I learned
content: The hardest product decision isn't what to build. It's what to stop building. OneCashless had momentum, internal supporters, and a head start. It also wasn't going to work. Parking it early saved months of engineering time and prevented a second payments crisis.

The second lesson: when the technical problem seems unsolvable, the answer is usually in the data model, not in the UI. The double-counting problem disappeared once I stopped looking for a user-facing fix and started mapping where money actually flows in the system.

The third: choosing a partner isn't a vendor evaluation. It's a product decision. Amusement Connect won not because they had the best pitch, but because their architecture solved a problem ours couldn't solve alone.

cta.text: These cases tell the how. For the who, there's the about page. If you want to talk, write me.

lab.headline: Same thinking, applied to my own problems.
lab.intro: Every project here started from a real need, not a tutorial. Same approach as the day job: understand the problem, build the minimum to validate the idea, decide what not to build. Code is the means. Product thinking is the point.

casahunter.title: CasaHunter
casahunter.description: A tool that apartment-hunts for you, learns from your feedback, and pings you on Telegram.
casahunter.whatItTaughtMe: Three-pass scoring: filter 80% deterministically, spend AI budget on the 20% that matters. Same logic applicable to any product at scale.

moneymind.title: MoneyMind
moneymind.description: Personal finance that doesn't ask you to fill out spreadsheets. Automatic transaction analysis, pattern recognition, weekly insights. In development.
moneymind.whatItTaughtMe: Designing for people who hate spreadsheets means the UI can't look like one either.

openclaw.title: OpenClaw
openclaw.description: Open-source web scraper for extracting structured product data from e-commerce sites. Experiment phase.
openclaw.whatItTaughtMe: Structured extraction at scale requires knowing when to use headless browsers and when a simple HTTP request is enough.

cta.text: If you're curious about how I think when I build, the professional case studies tell the same story at a different scale.

approach.headline: How I make product decisions.
approach.intro: No framework with a name. A way of working that formed through being a designer, then a developer, then a PM. Each phase changed how the problems look. What follows isn't a methodology. It's what happens when there's a decision to make about what to build.

s1.title: 1. Understand before you touch anything
s1.text:
The temptation when you arrive on a struggling product is to act immediately. Prove you're doing something. At QubicaAMF the payment system was in crisis. The first decision: don't touch anything for two weeks.

That's not inertia. It's the time to figure out which of the 44 open problems are actually costing customers money right now, and which can wait. The difference between "urgent" and "important" isn't visible on day one. It becomes visible after talking to support, reading the tickets, and looking at the data.

The result: 44 items, 12 critical, 4 with immediate financial impact. The rest could wait. Without those two weeks, the work would have been on everything simultaneously. With them, the 4 most severe resolved in 6 weeks.

s2.title: 2. The problem is in the funnel, not where you think
s2.text:
Almost always, the first diagnosis is wrong. Not from incompetence. Because whoever asks you to solve the problem sees the symptom, not the cause.

At QubicaAMF a center was about to cancel the integration because they couldn't reconcile their books. Every transaction appeared as "uncategorized income" in Square. The internal diagnosis was "partner problem, not solvable on our end." Tracing the data model revealed an existing API field that solved the problem without architectural changes. The center stayed and became a reference account.

At LeadsBridge the bridge mapping configuration flow was generating errors on repeat. Every error became a ticket. Support volume grew proportionally with new users. The problem wasn't ticket volume. It was the multi-step flow generating them. The redesign cut setup time by <bold>35%</bold>.

The pattern: before building anything, verify you're solving the right problem. It costs less than any sprint.

s3.title: 3. Build to discover, not to deliver
s3.text:
An idea, before becoming a ticket, becomes a working prototype. Not a mockup. Code you can touch.

CasaHunter was born this way: needing an apartment, no portal that worked, a scraper written over a weekend. But the interesting product decision wasn't building it. It was the 3-pass scoring architecture: first pass deterministic (free), second pass AI only on promising listings, third pass adaptive based on feedback. Same logic applicable to an enterprise product: don't send everything to AI. Filter first, spend where it counts.

Prototyping in code reveals things no spec document would have told you. Technical constraints that change the product. Edge cases that change the priority. Costs that change the strategy.

s4.title: 4. Know what not to build
s4.text:
The hardest decision isn't what to build. It's what to stop building.

In cashless, OneCashless was the obvious candidate. Internal product, organizational support, head start. But discovery showed the product didn't hold up. The business model was unclear, the architecture required work that couldn't be justified. Parked. Deliberately.

In the payments rescue, 23 of 44 items shipped. The other 21 were deliberate "not now" decisions, each with a documented rationale. When the refund feature wasn't solid enough before the holiday peak, it was pulled back. A full testing day rather than shipping broken logic to hundreds of centers during their busiest week.

A documented "no" is a deliverable just as much as shipped code.

s5.title: 5. Communicate as if you're not in the room
s5.text:
The best work is useless if nobody understands it. Especially in remote, multi-timezone, multi-country contexts.

The default: structured weekly recaps, operational memos for urgent decisions, meeting minutes published within hours. When the support team needed to help customers with issues that weren't fixed yet, video tutorials with workarounds filled the gap. Bridge solutions while the real fixes were in the pipeline.

That's not bureaucracy. It's how a distributed team maintains context without having to ask "what's going on?" every Monday morning.

closing.text:
There's no name for this approach. It's the result of having designed interfaces, written code, and managed products for 10+ years. Each phase added a different way of looking at the same problem.

If you want to see how it works in practice, the case studies have the details.

cta.text: If you want to see how it works in practice, the case studies have the details.
